content: The masses of block $$A$$ and $$B$$ are $$m$$ and $$M$$, respectively. Between $$A$$ and $$B$$, there is a constant frictional force $$F$$ and $$B$$ can slide on a smooth horizontal surface. A is set in motion with velocity $$3 v_0$$ while $$B$$ is at rest. What is the distance moved by $$A$$ relative to $$B$$ before they move with the same velocity?
explanation:
Consider the FBD as given below.

Equations of motion,

$$\begin{aligned} & a_A=\frac{F}{m}(\text { in }-x \text { direction }) \\ & a_B=\frac{F}{M}(\text { in }+x \text { direction }) \end{aligned}$$

Thus, we have, relative accleration of $$A$$ w.r.t. $$B$$ as

$$\begin{aligned} a_{A B} & =a_A-a_B=\frac{-F}{m}-\frac{F}{M} \\ & =-F\left(\frac{M+m}{M m}\right) \quad \text{(along} ~\mathrm{-x}-\text{direction)} \end{aligned}$$

Initial relative velocities of $$A$$ w.r.t $$B, u_{A B}=3 v_0$$ using equation $$v^2-u^2+2 a s$$,

$$\begin{aligned} 0 & =9 v_0^2-\frac{2 F(M+m)}{M m} s \\ \Rightarrow \quad s & =\frac{9 M m v_0^2}{2 F(M+m)} \end{aligned}$$

Distance moved relative to $$B, S_{A B}=\frac{9 M m v_0^2}{2 F(M+m)}$$

content: The initial speed of a body of mass 2.0 kg is 5 m/s. A force acts for 4 s in the direction of motion of the body, as shown in force-time graph. The impulse of force is
explanation:
Impulse of a force = Area between the force-time graph

Hence, impulse of force = area of $$\Delta$$OA'A + area of rectangle A'B'BA + area of trapezium BB'C'C + area of rectangle CC'ED

$$ = {1 \over 2} \times 1.5 \times 3 + 1 \times 3 + {1 \over 2}(3 + 2)(3 - 2.5) + 2 \times 1$$

$$ = 2.25 + 3 + 1.25 + 2$$

$$ = 8.50$$ Ns

content:
A uniform rod AB of length L = 1 m is sliding along two mutually perpendicular surfaces OP and OQ as shown in the figure.

When the rod subtends an angle $$\theta$$ = 30$$^\circ$$ with OQ, the end B has a velocity $$\sqrt3$$ m/s. The velocity of end A at that time is
explanation:
According to the question,

Let OB = x, OA = y and x = L cos$$\theta$$

So, by Pythagoras theorem,

$$\Rightarrow$$ x2 + y2 = L2

Differentiating w.r.t. 't', we get

$$2x{{dx} \over {dt}} + 2y{{dy} \over {dt}} = 0$$

$$2x{v_B} + 2y{v_A} = 0$$ [$$\because$$ $${{dx} \over {dt}} = {v_B}$$ and $${{dy} \over {dt}} = {v_A}$$]

$$ \Rightarrow {v_A} = - {x \over y}{v_B}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left| {{v_A}} \right| = {x \over y}{v_B} = {v_B}\cot \theta $$ [$$\because$$ $${x \over y} = \cot \theta $$]

$$ = \sqrt 3 \times \cot 30^\circ $$

$$ = \sqrt 3 \times {{\sqrt 3 }}$$ = 3 m/s

content: A block of mass 10 kg rests on a rough inclined plane making an angle of 30$$^\circ$$ with the horizontal. The coefficient of static friction between the block and the plane is 0.1. The friction force on the block is
explanation:
To find the friction force on the block, we first need to analyze the forces acting on the block on an inclined plane.

The forces acting on the block include:

  • Gravitational force (weight) acting vertically downward, $$mg$$
  • Normal force, $$N$$, exerted by the plane perpendicular to its surface
  • Frictional force, which acts parallel to the inclined surface and opposite to the direction of possible motion

The gravitational force can be resolved into two components:

  • Perpendicular to the inclined plane: $$mg \cos \theta$$
  • Parallel to the inclined plane: $$mg \sin \theta$$

In this problem:

  • Mass of the block, $$m$$ = 10 kg
  • Angle of the incline, $$\theta$$ = 30$$^\circ$$
  • Coefficient of static friction, $$\mu_s$$ = 0.1

First, we calculate the perpendicular component of the gravitational force to find the normal force:

$$N = mg \cos \theta$$

Substituting the values:

$$N = (10 \, \text{kg}) (9.8 \, \text{m/s}^2) \cos 30^\circ$$

$$\cos 30^\circ = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

$$N = 10 \times 9.8 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

$$N = 49 \sqrt{3} \, \text{N}$$

Next, we find the maximum static friction force:

$$f_{\text{max}} = \mu_s N$$

$$f_{\text{max}} = 0.1 \times 49 \sqrt{3} \, \text{N}$$

$$f_{\text{max}} = 4.9 \sqrt{3} \, \text{N}$$

We now compare this with the component of the gravitational force parallel to the inclined plane:

$$mg \sin \theta$$

Substituting the values:

$$mg \sin 30^\circ = 10 \times 9.8 \times \frac{1}{2}$$

$$mg \sin 30^\circ = 49 \, \text{N}$$

The friction force will be equal to the component of the gravitational force parallel to the inclined plane but within the limit of the maximum static friction force. Here, we recognize that the maximum static friction force (4.9 $$\sqrt{3}$$ N) is indeed greater than 4.9 N (the component of the gravitational force parallel to the incline). Hence, the actual friction force counteracting the motion will be:

$$49 \, \text{N}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C:

Option C: 49 N

content: When a body is dropped from a height h, then it hits the ground with a momentum p. If the same body is dropped from a height which is three times more than previous height, the percentage change in momentum when it hits the ground is
explanation:
Initial momentum, Pi = mv

$${P_i} = m\sqrt {2gh} $$ [$$\because$$ $$v\sqrt {2gh} $$]

New height of body, $${h_1} = h + 3h = 4h$$

Now, final momentum,

$${P_f} = m\sqrt {2g\,.\,{h_1}} $$

$$ = m\sqrt {2g(4h)} $$

$$ = m\,.\,2\sqrt {2gh} = 2{P_i}$$

$$\therefore$$ % change in momentum = $${{{P_f} - {P_i}} \over {{P_i}}} \times 100$$

$$ = {{2{P_i} - {P_i}} \over {{P_i}}} \times 100$$

$$ = 100\% $$

content: A plane requires for take off a speed of 72 kmh$$-$$1, the run on the ground being 50 m. The mass of the plane is 10000 kg and the coefficient of friction between the plane and the ground is 0.2. Assume that the plane accelerates uniformly during take off. The minimum force required by the engine of the plane for take off is
explanation:
Given, mass of plane, M = 10000 kg

Coefficient of friction, $$\mu$$ = 0.2

Distance, s = 50 m

Initial speed, u = 0

Final speed, v = 72 kmh$$-$$1 = 20 ms$$-$$1

Let a be the acceleration of the plane.

From third equation of motion,

v2 $$-$$ u2 = 2as

(20)2 $$-$$ (0)2 = 2 $$\times$$ a $$\times$$ 50

$$\therefore$$ $$a = {{20 \times 20} \over {100}} = 4$$ ms$$-$$2

Force required to provide necessary acceleration to the plane,

F1 = ma = 10000 $$\times$$ 4 = 4 $$\times$$ 104 N

Force required to overcome friction by engine,

F2 = $$\mu$$R = $$\mu$$mg = 0.2 $$\times$$ 10000 $$\times$$ 9.8

= 1.96 $$\times$$ 104 N

Therefore, the minimum force required by the engine,

F1 + F2 = 4 $$\times$$ 104 + 1.96 $$\times$$ 104 = 5.96 $$\times$$ 104 N
